metadata.threshold: An event with >= 4/6 features PRESENT exhibits classic bubble dynamics

mass_retail_participation.label: HIGH
mass_retail_participation.detail: Number of online brokerage accounts grew from 1.5M (1997) to 9.7M (2000)
mass_retail_participation.source: [CITATION NEEDED - SEC/FINRA data on online brokerage growth]

leverage_amplification.label: HIGH
leverage_amplification.detail: NYSE margin debt peaked at $278.5B in March 2000, up from $141B in 1997

exit_liquidity.label: HIGH
exit_liquidity.detail: 486 IPOs in 1999, 406 in 2000. Average first-day return 71% in 1999

speculative_disconnect.label: HIGH
speculative_disconnect.detail: Median P/E of NASDAQ stocks exceeded 100 at peak; many IPOs had no revenue

rapid_unwind_mechanism.label: HIGH
rapid_unwind_mechanism.detail: NASDAQ fell 78% from peak (5,048) to trough (1,114) over 31 months

denial_reflexivity.label: HIGH
denial_reflexivity.detail: 'Housing prices never decline nationally' was consensus. Bernanke (2005): 'no bubble'

mass_retail_participation.label: HIGH
mass_retail_participation.detail: Homeownership rate peaked at 69.2% (2004). Subprime loans ~20% of originations by 2006
mass_retail_participation.source: US Census Bureau; Inside Mortgage Finance

leverage_amplification.label: EXTREME
leverage_amplification.detail: Average LTV exceeded 80%; many loans 100%+ LTV. CDO notional values in trillions

exit_liquidity.label: HIGH
exit_liquidity.detail: MBS widely held by pension funds, banks, retail investors globally

speculative_disconnect.label: HIGH
speculative_disconnect.detail: Home prices 70% above historical trend relative to rents and income

rapid_unwind_mechanism.label: HIGH
rapid_unwind_mechanism.detail: S&P 500 fell 57% from Oct 2007 to Mar 2009. Cascading bank failures, AIG bailout

denial_reflexivity.label: HIGH
denial_reflexivity.detail: 'Japan as Number One' narrative. Imperial Palace grounds worth more than all California real estate
denial_reflexivity.source: Vogel (1979), Japan as Number One; widely cited comparison

mass_retail_participation.label: HIGH
mass_retail_participation.detail: Japanese household stock ownership peaked; land speculation widespread among individuals
mass_retail_participation.source: [CITATION NEEDED - Bank of Japan financial statistics]

leverage_amplification.label: EXTREME
leverage_amplification.detail: Bank lending grew ~10% annually 1985-1989. Land used as collateral for further land purchases

exit_liquidity.label: HIGH
exit_liquidity.detail: Tokyo Stock Exchange was world's largest by market cap in 1989. Highly liquid public markets
exit_liquidity.source: World Federation of Exchanges historical data

speculative_disconnect.label: HIGH
speculative_disconnect.detail: Nikkei P/E exceeded 70 at peak. Land prices 5-10x reasonable valuations
speculative_disconnect.source: [CITATION NEEDED - BOJ land price survey data]

rapid_unwind_mechanism.label: MODERATE
rapid_unwind_mechanism.detail: Nikkei fell ~60% by 1992 but full recovery took 30+ years (slow deflation, not panic crash)

denial_reflexivity.label: MIXED
denial_reflexivity.detail: Crypto community actively trained members to dismiss 'FUD'. But external skepticism was high

mass_retail_participation.label: HIGH
mass_retail_participation.detail: Coinbase had 98M verified users by Q4 2021. NFT market dominated by retail

leverage_amplification.label: MODERATE
leverage_amplification.detail: Significant DeFi leverage and exchange margin trading, but less than traditional finance

exit_liquidity.label: HIGH
exit_liquidity.detail: Crypto markets operate 24/7 with near-instant settlement. Highly liquid for BTC/ETH

speculative_disconnect.label: HIGH
speculative_disconnect.detail: NFT market reached $25B in 2021 sales. Many projects had no utility beyond speculation

rapid_unwind_mechanism.label: HIGH
rapid_unwind_mechanism.detail: BTC fell 77% (2021 cycle). NFT floor prices collapsed 90%+. FTX/Luna/3AC cascading failures
rapid_unwind_mechanism.source: Market data; public reporting on FTX collapse

denial_reflexivity.label: ABSENT
denial_reflexivity.reasoning: Public discourse widely questions AI valuations. 'AI bubble' is mainstream media narrative. Contrast with dot-com ('new economy') and housing ('prices never fall') where denial was dominant. Per the heuristic: if many people are calling it a bubble, it probably isn't one, because real bubbles require widespread denial to sustain overvaluation.

mass_retail_participation.label: ABSENT
mass_retail_participation.reasoning: No mechanism for broad retail AI-specific investment. Major AI companies are private. Public companies with AI exposure (NVDA, MSFT) are diversified. Dot-com had 486 IPOs in 1999 alone; AI has had essentially zero pure-play IPOs.

leverage_amplification.label: PARTIAL
leverage_amplification.reasoning: VC leverage exists (fund-of-funds, SPVs). But no consumer leverage (cf. mortgages in 2008) and no derivative multiplication (cf. CDOs). Leverage is contained within sophisticated institutional investors who understand the risk, not distributed to retail.

exit_liquidity.label: ABSENT
exit_liquidity.reasoning: Private company shares are illiquid. No public market for mass simultaneous exit. WeWork demonstrated the fizzle-not-pop pattern: private overvaluations just get written down. For a crash, you need the ability to sell at scale, which requires public markets.

speculative_disconnect.label: PARTIAL
speculative_disconnect.reasoning: Some AI valuations may exceed near-term revenue potential (OpenAI at 100x+ revenue). But unlike dot-com, the underlying technology demonstrably works and generates real revenue. The question is WHICH companies capture value, not WHETHER the technology has value.

rapid_unwind_mechanism.label: ABSENT
rapid_unwind_mechanism.reasoning: No cascade mechanism. GPU assets can't be liquidated at scale (market would collapse). Companies with compute infrastructure will restructure, not evaporate. Even severely distressed AI companies look like utilities in bankruptcy, not dot-com zeros.

summary.conclusion: AI investment does NOT exhibit the structural features of a classic asset bubble. It scores 1.0/6.0 on structural features, compared to an average of 5.62/6.0 for confirmed historical bubbles. The absence of mass retail participation, exit liquidity, and rapid unwind mechanisms means classical bubble crash dynamics cannot manifest in the current market structure.
